questions.question_text: In Maine, when two unrelated buyers take title together without specifying the form of ownership, what type of tenancy is presumed?
distractors: Joint tenancy with survivorship
Tenancy by the entirety
Community property ownership
questions.explanation: Maine law defaults to tenancy in common when the deed does not specify the form of co-ownership. Unlike joint tenancy, tenancy in common carries no right of survivorship, and each owner's share passes through their estate at death. Joint tenancy must be expressly created with survivorship language.

questions.question_text: A married couple asks their Maine agent to have the deed to their new home run to them 'as tenants by the entirety' so that one spouse's individual creditors cannot reach it. What is the legal effect of that language in a Maine deed?
questions.correct_answer: It creates a joint tenancy, not a tenancy by the entirety
distractors: It creates a tenancy by the entirety with creditor protection
It makes the deed void for uncertainty
It creates community property with survivorship
questions.explanation: Maine has not recognized tenancy by the entirety since 1844 (Palmer v. Flint). Under 33 M.R.S. Section 159, deed language such as 'as tenants by the entirety' creates a joint tenancy with right of survivorship. There is no entirety-style creditor protection in Maine, and Maine is not a community property state.

questions.question_text: Two business partners want to hold Maine property so that if one dies, the survivor automatically receives the deceased partner's share. What must their deed include?
distractors: A notarized partnership agreement
Community property designation
No special language is needed
questions.explanation: Maine requires express language creating the right of survivorship to establish a joint tenancy. Without that specific language, the deed creates a tenancy in common by default, and each partner's share would pass through their estate rather than automatically to the survivor.

questions.question_text: A divorcing couple in Maine disputes how to divide their jointly owned home. Which legal principle governs the division?
distractors: Equal 50/50 community property split
Automatic survivorship partition
Homestead exemption allocation
questions.explanation: Maine follows equitable distribution, not community property, when dividing marital assets in divorce. The court considers factors such as each spouse's contributions, economic circumstances, and the length of the marriage to reach a fair but not necessarily equal division.

questions.question_text: A Maine deed conveys a home to a married couple 'as tenants by the entirety.' A creditor later obtains a judgment against the husband alone for a business debt. What is the creditor's position?
questions.correct_answer: The creditor can reach the husband's interest; the deed created a joint tenancy
distractors: Nothing can be reached because entirety property is fully shielded
The creditor takes nothing because the deed is void
The creditor can force a sale of both spouses' interests
questions.explanation: Maine does not recognize tenancy by the entirety — and has not since 1844 (Palmer v. Flint) — so 'entirety' creditor protection does not exist in Maine. Under 33 M.R.S. Section 159, entirety language in a deed creates a joint tenancy with right of survivorship. A judgment creditor of one spouse can therefore reach that spouse's joint tenancy interest, though not the other spouse's interest.

questions.question_text: Which type of deed provides the greatest protection to a buyer in Maine?
distractors: Quitclaim deed without any covenant
Quitclaim deed with covenant
Municipal quitclaim deed
questions.explanation: A warranty deed provides the buyer maximum protection. Under Maine's Short Form Deeds Act (33 M.R.S. Section 763), the grantor covenants lawful seizin in fee, freedom from all encumbrances, good right to sell and convey, and that the grantor will warrant and defend title against the lawful claims of all persons. This is the strongest of Maine's statutory short form deeds.

questions.question_text: A seller conveys property using a quitclaim deed with covenant. What does the grantor warrant?
questions.correct_answer: The grantor has not encumbered title
distractors: Full title free of all prior defects
Nothing at all about the title status
That all prior owners had clear title
questions.explanation: The quitclaim with covenant is a distinctive New England instrument. The grantor warrants only that they personally have not encumbered the property during their ownership. Unlike a warranty deed, it provides no warranty about the actions of prior owners or the overall state of the title chain.

questions.question_text: A Maine seller agrees to convey whatever interest she may have in a disputed strip of land, making no promises about the state of the title. Which Maine statutory short form deed fits this transaction?
distractors: Warranty deed
Quitclaim deed with covenant
Mortgage deed
questions.explanation: The Quitclaim Deed Without Covenant, also called a release deed (33 M.R.S. Sections 775 and 161), conveys whatever interest the grantor has with no title covenants whatsoever. Maine's statutory short form deeds include the warranty deed, quitclaim deed with covenant, quitclaim deed without covenant (release deed), mortgage deed, and municipal quitclaim deed, among other fiduciary and mortgage-related forms — none is a 'merger deed.'

questions.question_text: A father gifts his lakefront cabin to his daughter. She asks what methods can legally transfer ownership. Which is NOT a valid transfer method in this situation?
distractors: Gift deed from father to daughter
Devise through the father's will
Quitclaim deed from father to daughter
questions.explanation: When a father voluntarily gifts property to his daughter, adverse possession does not apply. Adverse possession requires hostile possession against the owner's wishes. Valid voluntary transfers include gift deed, quitclaim deed, or devise through a will. The daughter cannot adversely possess property her father willingly gives.

questions.question_text: A grantor signs a deed in Maine but does not have it acknowledged before a notary. What is the effect on recording?
questions.correct_answer: The Registry of Deeds will reject it
distractors: It records normally without issue
It records but has a 90-day grace period
It transfers title but with reduced priority
questions.explanation: Maine requires that all deeds be acknowledged before a notary public (or other authorized official) before they can be recorded at the county Registry of Deeds. Without proper acknowledgment, the registry will reject the document, leaving the grantee without the protection that recording provides.

questions.question_text: How many consecutive years must a person occupy property to claim adverse possession in Maine?
distractors: 7 years
10 years
15 years
questions.explanation: Maine requires 20 years of continuous adverse possession to acquire title. The possession must be actual, open, notorious, exclusive, hostile, and continuous for the entire 20-year period. This is among the longer statutory periods in the United States.

questions.question_text: A neighbor has used a strip of your Maine property as a garden for 18 years openly and without permission. She now claims ownership by adverse possession. What is the likely outcome?
questions.correct_answer: The claim fails for insufficient time
distractors: The claim succeeds if she proves hostility
The claim succeeds after 15 years
The claim transfers only garden rights
questions.explanation: Maine requires 20 continuous years of adverse possession. At 18 years, even if all other elements are met (actual, open, notorious, exclusive, hostile, continuous), the neighbor falls 2 years short of the statutory period. The true owner still has time to assert their rights and stop the clock.

questions.question_text: Under Maine's recording statute (33 M.R.S. Section 201), an unrecorded deed is still effective against which of the following?
questions.correct_answer: A person who had actual notice of the unrecorded deed
distractors: A later purchaser for value with no notice of the deed
Anyone who searches the registry after the sale
No one — an unrecorded deed is void for all purposes
questions.explanation: Under 33 M.R.S. Section 201, an unrecorded conveyance is not effective against any person except the grantor, the grantor's heirs and devisees, and persons having actual notice of it. The deed still binds those parties, but a subsequent purchaser without actual notice takes free of an unrecorded deed — which is why prompt recording at the county Registry of Deeds is essential.

questions.question_text: In Maine, recording fees at the Registry of Deeds are based on what factor?
distractors: The sale price of the property
The assessed value of the property
The number of pages in the document
questions.explanation: Effective January 1, 2026 (PL 2025, c. 328), Maine registries of deeds charge a flat recording fee per instrument — $35 for private parties ($25 for the State or a municipality) — plus a $5 records-preservation surcharge, or $40 total for a typical document regardless of length. This replaced the former per-page schedule ($19 for the first page plus $2 per additional page, plus a $3 surcharge). Fees are set by statute (33 M.R.S. Sections 751-752) and are not tied to property value.

questions.question_text: Seller A conveys to Buyer B, who does not record. Later, Seller A fraudulently conveys the same property to Buyer C, who has no knowledge of the prior sale and records immediately. Under Maine's recording system, who holds title?
questions.correct_answer: Buyer C, who recorded without notice
distractors: Buyer B, who received the first deed only
Seller A, because both deeds are now void
Both buyers share title as tenants in common
questions.explanation: Under 33 M.R.S. Section 201, an unrecorded deed is not effective against anyone except the grantor, the grantor's heirs and devisees, and persons with actual notice of it. Buyer C prevails because C purchased without actual notice of B's unrecorded deed; had C actually known of B's deed, C would not be protected. C's prompt recording also protects C against any later claimants — which is why prompt recording is critical to protecting ownership rights.
